statement: Under the EEA Agreement, agriculture is largely excluded from free movement of goods. EEA Article 19 provides for periodic reviews to progressively liberalise agricultural trade, but Iceland and Norway have maintained very high tariffs on key products: Iceland applies tariffs of 30–100%+ on dairy, meat, and some vegetables. Under full EU membership, all internal tariffs would be eliminated, and Iceland would adopt the EU's Common External Tariff. This would expose Icelandic dairy and lamb producers to competition from EU producers with larger scale and lower costs. However, Iceland would also gain tariff-free access to the EU's 450-million consumer market for its agricultural exports (currently minimal). The key difference: EEA allows Iceland to protect its agricultural market; EU membership would not, except through negotiated transition periods and possible national aid provisions.
source_name: EEA Agreement Article 19; Icelandic Ministry for Foreign Affairs — EEA review documentation
caveats: The practical impact depends heavily on transition periods and any special provisions Iceland might negotiate. Iceland's high production costs (small scale, harsh climate, expensive labour) mean that even with full market access, Icelandic agricultural exports would remain small. The main impact would be on import competition, not export opportunities.

related_entries.statement: The EU Common Agricultural Policy (CAP) provides direct payments to farmers, rural development support, and market intervention measures. The CAP budget for 2021–2027 is approximately €387 billion, making it the largest single item in the EU budget (~31%). Iceland's agricultural sector would be eligible for CAP support upon EU membership.

Icelandic agricultural subsidies totalled approximately ISK 20–22 billion (roughly €130–145 million) per year as of 2024, primarily through direct payments to sheep and dairy farmers. This equates to approximately 55–60% of gross farm revenue being derived from government support, making Iceland one of the most heavily subsidised agricultural sectors in the OECD. The OECD Producer Support Estimate (PSE) for Iceland was 54% in 2023, compared to the EU average of approximately 19%.

As of 2025, approximately 70% of EU internal market legislation has been incorporated into Icelandic law through the EEA Agreement. This covers free movement of goods, services, capital, and persons, but excludes fisheries, agriculture, and justice/home affairs.

citations.report_title: Landbúnaðarstefna ESB henti íslenskum landbúnaði illa

claims.claim_text: Frjálst flæði landbúnaðarvara hentar illa íslenskum aðstæðum.

citations.report_title: Þrír hópar sem myndu hagnast mest á aðild Íslands að Evrópusambandinu

claims.claim_text: Vöruverð á Íslandi myndi lækka við ESB-aðild vegna þess að allur innflutningur frá ESB-löndum yrði tollfrjáls.
